title: Cat or Kitten Straining to Urinate: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your cat is straining to urinate, it is a high-priority medical emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care is essential to save their life.
content_html:
TL;DR: Straining to urinate is a life-threatening emergency in cats that requires immediate veterinary attention to prevent kidney failure or death. This behavior often signals a painful urinary blockage, especially in males, which can become fatal within 24 to 48 hours.

What does it mean if my cat is straining to urinate?

Straining to urinate, also known as dysuria or stranguria, occurs when a cat or kitten frequently visits the litter box, stays in a squatting position for an extended period, or vocalizes in pain while attempting to pee. This behavior often indicates an underlying issue such as inflammation, crystals, or a physical blockage in the urinary tract.

Is my cat straining to pee a medical emergency?

  • Straining to urinate is a High Urgency emergency requiring immediate intervention.
  • In many cases, especially with male cats, this indicates a urethral obstruction (a "blocked cat").
  • If a cat cannot pass urine, toxins build up in the body rapidly, leading to kidney failure, bladder rupture, or fatal heart rhythm changes within 24 to 48 hours.
  • You must contact an emergency veterinarian immediately if you notice your cat straining.

Why should I take a photo or video of my cat straining for the vet?

  • A photo of the litter box can show the veterinary team if there are tiny spots of blood or a complete absence of urine.
  • A video of your cat's posture and vocalizations helps the triage nurse determine the level of distress.
  • Providing this information allows the clinic to prioritize your pet's care and prepare for your arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats and kittens, straining to urinate, or complete urethral obstruction, is an emergency requiring immediate treatment. Affected animals may frequently attempt to urinate, producing only a fine stream, a few drops, or nothing, and may exhibit extreme pain when attempting to urinate. Complete obstruction leads to uremia within 36-48 hours, resulting in depression, anorexia, vomiting, diarrhea, dehydration, coma, and death within 48-72 hours. Clinical signs of partial obstruction include dribbling blood-tinged urine after prolonged, painful (stranguria) efforts to urinate. With complete obstruction, animals may exhibit tenesmus, tail twitching, and weight shifting. Hyperkalemia and metabolic acidosis are life-threatening complications. Initial emergency care involves immediate relief of obstruction by catheterization and fluid therapy with normal saline.

Chapter: Urology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1527)

faqs.question: Can I wait until morning to take my cat to the vet?
faqs.answer: No. If your cat is unable to urinate, their condition can become life-threatening in a matter of hours. This requires immediate emergency intervention regardless of the time of day.

faqs.question: My cat is still passing tiny drops of urine; is it still an emergency?
faqs.answer: Yes. Passing only tiny drops often indicates a partial blockage or severe inflammation, both of which can progress to a full, fatal obstruction very quickly.

faqs.question: How can I tell if it is constipation or a urinary issue?
faqs.answer: It is often very difficult for owners to tell the difference as the straining posture is similar. Because a urinary blockage is much more rapidly fatal than constipation, you should always treat straining as a urinary emergency until a vet proves otherwise.
